questions.text: Which 2 characteristics of radiofrequency electrosurgery distinguish it from electrocautery? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Requires direct tissue contact with a heated element
B) Causes intracellular ion oscillation
C) Transfers current directly from a heated tip
D) Converts electrical current to thermal energy via resistance
E) Operates with alternating polarity

questions.explanation: Unlike electrocautery, RF electrosurgery uses alternating polarity and causes intracellular ion oscillation, which creates friction and heat. Electrocautery involves a heated element making contact with tissue.

questions.text: Which of the following techniques are appropriate to achieve effective coagulation of a 2 mm vessel using monopolar energy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Direct tissue contact with the electrode
B) Use of coag waveform with spray mode
C) Tissue compression prior to activation
D) Application of low-voltage continuous current
E) Rapid sweeping motion with the electrode

questions.explanation: Effective coagulation requires contact, compression, and controlled heating. Spray mode and rapid sweeping motions reduce energy transfer and can increase lateral spread.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about bipolar vessel sealing are correct? (Select 3 best answers).

answerOptions.text: A) High-voltage coagulation waveforms are optimal for deep sealing
B) Compression helps align vessel walls for better seal
C) Water vapor contributes to tissue dissection
D) Electrode surface area affects sealing precision
E) Seal failure may occur if temperature rise is too rapid

questions.explanation: Effective vessel sealing requires compression, appropriate electrode geometry, and controlled temperature rise. High-voltage waveforms increase collateral damage; water vapor does not improve sealing.

questions.text: Which factors increase the risk of capacitive coupling during laparoscopic surgery? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Monopolar energy used with metal cannulas
B) Low-voltage continuous current
C) Activation in open circuit
D) Reusable bipolar grasper with cracked insulation
E) Use of plastic trocar with internal metal shaft

questions.explanation: Capacitive coupling risk increases with metal cannulas, open-circuit activation, and plastic trocars that contain internal metal conductors.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly describe cavitation during ultrasonic energy application? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) It occurs at sub-boiling temperatures
B) It results from ultrasonic blade oscillation
C) It causes intracellular vaporization
D) It is unique to bipolar radiofrequency devices
E) It contributes to both dissection and coagulation

questions.explanation: Cavitation is driven by ultrasonic vibration and pressure changes that form vapor bubbles at sub-boiling temperatures, aiding dissection and coagulation. It is not an RF-specific effect.

questions.text: At what temperature range and current configuration does protein denaturation and coagulation typically occur during electrosurgery?

answerOptions.text: A) 60–95°C using low-voltage, focused current
B) 60–95°C using low-voltage, unfocused current
C) ≥100°C using high-voltage, focused current
D) ≥100°C using high-voltage, unfocused current

questions.explanation: Protein denaturation/coagulation occur around 60–95°C, achieved with low-voltage, unfocused current that promotes gradual heating and broader thermal spread.

questions.text: In a monopolar electrosurgical circuit, which of the following completes the circuit by allowing current to safely exit the patient’s body and return to the electrosurgical generator?

answerOptions.text: A) Active electrode tip
B) Bipolar forceps
C) Dispersive electrode
D) Surgical instrument handle

questions.explanation: The dispersive (return) electrode provides a safe, low-resistance pathway back to the generator to complete the circuit.

questions.text: Why is a low-voltage waveform preferred for precise electrosurgical cutting?

answerOptions.text: A) It produces rapid heating with minimal lateral thermal spread
B) It increases cavitation
C) It enables capacitive coupling for better current delivery
D) It reduces the power output to zero

questions.explanation: Low-voltage, continuous current yields smooth cutting with limited lateral spread; high-voltage modulation favors coagulation/charring.

questions.text: At approximately what tissue temperature does vaporization occur during electrosurgery?
questions.explanation: Vaporization occurs when water becomes steam—at temperatures above 100°C—causing tissue disruption.
questions.submodule: Module 4.2 – Effect of Electrosurgery on Tissue

questions.text: Which factor most directly increases the power density (or concentration) of the electrosurgical effect?

answerOptions.text: A) Output frequency
B) Voltage mode
C) Electrode surface area
D) Duration of activation

questions.explanation: Smaller electrode surface area concentrates power into a smaller region, raising power density.
questions.submodule: Module 4.2 – Effect of Electrosurgery on Tissue

questions.text: Which configuration would best minimize lateral thermal spread during vessel sealing?

answerOptions.text: A) Wide electrode, high voltage output
B) Narrow electrode, low voltage continuous current
C) Spray coagulation with blend waveform
D) Non-contact waveform with high peak voltage

questions.explanation: Focused geometry plus low-voltage continuous current yields controlled heating and minimal spread.
questions.submodule: Module 4.2 – Effect of Electrosurgery on Tissue

questions.text: What is a defining feature of bipolar electrosurgical instruments?

answerOptions.text: A) They require a dispersive electrode placed on the patient
B) They use ultrasound rather than electricity
C) Both electrodes are incorporated into the instrument itself
D) They can only be used for superficial tissue ablation

questions.explanation: Bipolar devices complete the circuit locally between their two jaws; no dispersive pad is needed.

questions.text: Which parameter most directly affects the quality of a vessel seal using bipolar energy?

answerOptions.text: A) Type of gas insufflation
B) Degree of tissue compression
C) Generator frequency setting
D) Patient body temperature

questions.explanation: Optimal compression apposes vessel walls and supports uniform coagulation for a durable seal.

questions.text: Why might a high-voltage modulated waveform lead to inadequate vessel sealing?

answerOptions.text: A) It cuts tissue too rapidly
B) It prevents water vapor formation
C) It can cause charring with preserved central lumen
D) It creates an open current loop

questions.explanation: High-voltage modulation can caramelize tissue superficially without fully sealing the vessel lumen.

questions.text: Which type of injury is least likely when using a bipolar device correctly?

answerOptions.text: A) Lateral thermal spread
B) Direct coupling
C) Heated instrument contact injury
D) Conduction via compressed tissue

questions.explanation: Direct coupling is a monopolar phenomenon; properly used bipolar systems localize current between jaws.

questions.text: What is the primary concern with insulation failure in monopolar instruments?

answerOptions.text: A) Smoke generation
B) Water vapor spread
C) Unintended current escape causing deep thermal injury
D) Deactivation delay

questions.explanation: Cracked insulation allows stray current to arc to adjacent tissues, causing occult burns.

questions.text: Capacitive coupling is most likely to occur in which of the following scenarios?

answerOptions.text: A) Bipolar coagulation of a 2 mm vessel
B) Reusable bipolar instrument with cracked insulation
C) Monopolar activation in a metal cannula during single-port surgery
D) Ultrasonic shears set to level 5 cutting mode

questions.explanation: With monopolar energy near conductive cannulas, RF fields can induce current through intact insulation to adjacent metal.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding ultrasonic surgical devices?

answerOptions.text: A) They generate heat via radiofrequency current
B) They are not associated with lateral thermal injury
C) They operate through mechanical oscillation at ultrasonic frequencies
D) They require a dispersive electrode for return current

questions.explanation: Ultrasonic devices convert electrical energy into mechanical oscillation (≈55 kHz) via piezoelectric crystals.

questions.text: Which tissue effect results from the pressure-induced vaporization of intracellular water at sub-boiling temperatures?

answerOptions.text: A) Thermal spread
B) Cavitation
C) Coaptation
D) Desiccation

questions.explanation: Cavitation arises from pressure changes around the oscillating blade, forming vapor bubbles at sub-boiling temperatures.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the thermal profile of ultrasonic blades compared to radiofrequency devices?

answerOptions.text: A) Ultrasonic blades cool more rapidly than RF instruments
B) RF instruments reach higher peak temperatures
C) Ultrasonic blades maintain temperatures >60°C for longer after deactivation
D) RF and ultrasonic instruments cannot exceed 50°C due to design limits

questions.explanation: Ultrasonic blades retain heat longer after deactivation and can remain >60°C, posing contact-burn risk.
